title: pierics.com — a Wordle clone, shipped
body:
New side project is live: pierics.com, my own take on Wordle. Guess the hidden word in six tries — type a guess, and each tile flips to tell you what's right: green for the correct letter in the correct spot, yellow for a letter that's in the word but somewhere else, grey for one that isn't there at all.

It's the kind of build I love — a small, self-contained game with tight rules and instant feedback, the whole thing running in the browser on its own domain. Quick to pick up, hard to put down. Go break your streak: pierics.com.

title: pangea8.com — all-new landing page, built end to end
body:
pangea8.com has a real face now. Until today the domain pointed straight at the test hub — functional, but it never told anyone what Pangea8 is. Today I shipped a full landing page and moved the hub to its own address (maqsudjon-cell.github.io/ielts-hub). The Start practicing button drops you straight into the tests.

The origin story is funny: I saw one of those viral "can you believe Claude did this" reels on Instagram and decided pangea8 deserved the same energy. The design went through three full iterations in one session with Claude. Version one was a cinematic renaissance museum — gold frames, Vermeer paintings, a rotating wax seal. Beautiful, but too luxury for an IELTS platform. Version two swung the other way: cream-and-coral minimalism. Clean, but boring and not our colors. Version three landed it: dark hi-tech in pangea8's own emerald, Linear/Vercel energy.

The hero is the heart of it. Aurora glow blobs drift behind an animated grid, the headline "Band 7.5 & beyond" decodes with a matrix-style scramble, and the centerpiece is a glass mock of the actual hub — browser chrome, search bar, filter pills, and a 3-column grid of real test cards (Mock Listening 76, CDI Reading 4...).

And the mock is alive. The search bar types by itself — "listening", then "reading" — the filter pills switch on their own, and the test cards dim and highlight to match, like someone is using the product right in front of you. A green tracer light runs around the card border, and on desktop the whole thing tilts in 3D as your cursor moves.

Then there's the ambient layer, my favorite part. Meteors streak across the hero every twenty-something seconds — and if you tap anywhere on the hero, a meteor fires from exactly where you touched. There are 38 twinkling stars, random grid cells that pulse green, and embers rising through the final CTA. Light mode gets its own character instead of just recoloring: the stars become soft green sparkles, meteors turn into deep-green comets, and floating ring-and-plus shapes drift around that only exist in light mode.

The theme system does both: it follows your device setting automatically, and a moon/sun toggle in the nav lets you override it — your choice is remembered, with zero flash on page load.

Sign-in is live too. Google auth through Firebase, and once you're signed in, Start practicing jumps you to the hub instantly — same tab, preconnected, prefetched. If the sign-in popup fails or you close it, you still go straight to the tests. Practice is never blocked. I also deferred the Firebase SDK so the page paints and animates instantly even on slow connections — the SDK loads in the background and attaches auth when it's ready.

Under the hood it's one 58KB HTML file. No frameworks, no build step — vanilla CSS and JS, Google Fonts, and Firebase over CDN. Spotlight hover cards, magnetic buttons, count-up stats, an animated band-score gauge, a scroll progress bar, a real favicon set (Google Search needs actual files, not data URIs), and a proper OG image for link previews.

The last piece was the domain migration. pangea8.com moved from the hub repo to the landing repo — before flipping it I checked every one of the 92 test links to make sure nothing would break (they all point to their own github.io pages, so nothing did). The hub now lives at maqsudjon-cell.github.io/ielts-hub and the brand finally lives at pangea8.com.

Next up: pushing past 100 tests. The landing already says 100+ — now I have to catch up to my own marketing.

title: IELTS Hub — 10 CDI Reading tests now live
body:
Added 6 more full Academic reading tests to the IELTS Hub (pangea8.com), bringing the total to 10 CDI Reading tests. CDI Reading 5 through CDI Reading 10 are now live alongside the earlier CDI 1 through 4.

Each test has 3 passages, 40 questions, and a 60-minute timer with instant scoring and answer checking. The hub now offers 16 reading tests total.

All built with vanilla HTML/CSS/JS, each running as its own standalone page.

title: Flarestamina — 40-Day IELTS Challenge tracker is live
body:
I launched Flarestamina (flarestamina.com) — a complete 40-Day IELTS Challenge tracker covering all four skills: Listening, Reading, Writing, and Speaking across 38 active days with 265 tasks. Users sign in with Google, check off tasks daily, and their progress auto-saves and syncs across devices in real time. The tracker includes a progress ring, a streak heatmap, and a class leaderboard.

I also built a gated Teacher Dashboard that lets me add or remove days from the plan, view individual student progress, and track the leaderboard. The whole thing runs on vanilla HTML/CSS/JS with Firebase v10 (Auth + Firestore) loaded over CDN — no frameworks, no build step. Deployed to GitHub Pages with a custom domain at flarestamina.com.

This is the first product I've shipped that other people can actually use to study. Building it taught me a lot about Firebase security rules, real-time sync, and shipping something that just works.

title: Pangeya Essay Platform — full overhaul
body:
Today I shipped a major update to the Pangeya Essay Platform. The goal was to make it faster, safer, and more solid all around.

The biggest change is a real-time onSnapshot listener. Essays now show up instantly after you submit, edit, or delete them — no more waiting around for a full Firestore re-query.

On the security side, I added XSS sanitization, so essay content is now cleaned before it's rendered to the screen.

I also fixed a handful of bugs: duplicate submissions are now prevented, the negative credits issue is resolved, duplicate functions were removed, and stale renderEssays() calls got cleaned up.

For accessibility, I added aria roles, the Escape key now closes modals, and Ctrl+Enter submits.

And finally, some visual polish: a favicon was added, along with a few responsive refinements.

Live at maqsudjon-cell.github.io/pangeya-essay-platform-/index-14.html and pangea8.com

title: IELTS Hub — 73 listening tests
body: Big update today! Added 35 new Diyorbek listening tests — Tests 41 through 76. That brings the total to 73 listening tests on the platform. Each test has 40 questions, full audio, and instant scoring. Check them out at pangea8.com

title: Launched pangea8.com
body: Today I officially launched pangea8.com — my IELTS practice hub. It hosts mock tests for Reading, Writing, Listening, and Speaking, plus the Pangeya Essay platform. Built with passion for students preparing for IELTS, but it's open to anyone preparing for IELTS. Setting up the domain through Cloudflare and connecting it to GitHub Pages took an afternoon. The hardest part was choosing the name.

title: IELTS Hub — 59 listening tests
body: Added 8 more Diyorbek listening tests — Tests 56 through 63. Fixed test ordering, added share buttons, and fixed Test 12 URL. Now at 59 total listening tests on the hub. Full audio, instant scoring, all on pangea8.com.

title: IELTS Hub — 10 new tests (31–40)
body:
Added 10 new Diyorbek listening tests to the hub today — Tests 31 through 40. That brings the total to 37 listening tests on the platform. Each test has 40 questions, full audio, and instant scoring.

Check them out at pangea8.com

title: IELTS Hub — 42 listening tests
body: Added 5 more Diyorbek listening tests — Tests 41 through 45. Full audio, instant scoring, all on pangea8.com.

title: IELTS Hub — 51 listening tests
body: Added 9 more Diyorbek listening tests — Tests 46 through 55 (except 49). Now at 51 total listening tests on the platform. Full audio, instant scoring, all on pangea8.com.

title: Why Pangea?
body: Pangea was the single supercontinent before everything drifted apart. I liked the metaphor — one world, one platform. The 8 in pangea8 stands for IELTS Band 8 — the goal most students aim for, and also looks like the infinity symbol when laid sideways. Pangea forever, Pangea eight. Brand names should mean something to the founder, even if nobody else gets it at first.

title: Making music from Tashkent
body: Releasing UK Garage tracks from Uzbekistan feels strange sometimes. I'm probably one of the only producers in Central Asia making this sound. My album HOME is out on Spotify under Matsumi. Most of my listeners are in the UK, which makes sense — that's where the genre lives. But it also tells me something: location doesn't matter anymore. The internet erased that.
